---
title: "Scatter Plot Widget Roadmap"
format: html
---

# ScatterPlotWidget Development Roadmap

This document tracks remaining development tasks for the Scatter Plot Widget. For documentation
of completed features and current architecture, see the [main developer documentation](index.qmd).

## Completed Work Summary

The following features are complete and documented in [index.qmd](index.qmd):

- **Widget skeleton**: EditorState pattern, registration, state/view/properties factories
- **State management**: `ScatterPlotState` with `HorizontalAxisState`, `VerticalAxisState` composition; `ScatterPlotStateData` serializable struct
- **Axis system**: `HorizontalAxisWidget` (X) and `VerticalAxisWidget` (Y) with bidirectional sync, including the "silent update" pattern
- **Interaction**: Separated X/Y zoom, pan via shared `PlotInteractionHelpers`
- **Properties panel**: Axis range controls in collapsible `Section` widgets; data source selection UI (key, column, offset per axis); reference line toggle; compatibility status label
- **OpenGL lifecycle**: `ScatterPlotOpenGLWidget` initialization, projection/view matrix management, pan/zoom mouse handlers
- **State serialization**: Full round-trip JSON via `rfl::json`
- **Registration wiring**: `timePositionSelected` ‚Üí `EditorRegistry::setCurrentTime()` connection
- **Data source descriptor**: `ScatterAxisSource` struct (Phase 1.1) defining data key, tensor column, and temporal offset
- **Row type compatibility validation** (Phase 1.3): `resolveSourceRowType()` and `checkSourceCompatibility()` functions in `Core/SourceCompatibility.hpp/.cpp` with full test coverage (21 test cases, 52 assertions)
- **Data source UI** (Phase 1.2): Key/column/offset combo boxes per axis with DataManager observer, compatibility label displaying `checkSourceCompatibility()` results
- **Point pair computation** (Phase 1.4): `buildScatterPoints()` factory function in `Core/BuildScatterPoints.hpp/.cpp` supporting all compatible source type combinations (ATS√óATS, ATS√óTensorTFI, TensorTFI√óATS, TensorTFI√óTensorTFI, Ordinal√óOrdinal, Interval√óInterval) with temporal offset and TimeFrameIndex join. Tested in `BuildScatterPoints.test.cpp`.
- **Point rendering** (Phase 1.5): SceneBuilder‚ÜíSceneRenderer pipeline using `addGlyphs()` for points (blue circles, size 5, alpha 0.8) with single instanced draw call
- **Auto-fit bounds** (Phase 1.6): Automatic X/Y bounds detection from data with 5% padding on first data load (when bounds are at defaults)
- **Reference line rendering** (Phase 1.7): $y = x$ diagonal via `addPolyLine()` with show/hide toggle in properties panel; endpoints update on pan/zoom
- **DataManager observer** (Phase 6 partial): Observer registration/removal in properties widget for combo box synchronization
- **Double-click-to-navigate** (Phase 2): Point hit testing via shared `SceneHitTester` + `QuadTree<EntityId>` spatial index, `pointDoubleClicked` ‚Üí `timePositionSelected` ‚Üí `EditorRegistry::setCurrentTime()` signal chain, yellow highlight glyph for navigated-to point

**Not yet implemented**: Glyph customization (Phase 4),
polygon selection and group integration (Phase 3), feature coloring (Phase 7),
stale key handling (Phase 6.3).

---

## Phase 1: Data Source Selection and Point Rendering

**Status**: Complete. All sub-phases (1.1‚Äì1.7) are implemented and tested.

**Goal**: Allow the user to select X and Y data sources from `AnalogTimeSeries` and `TensorData` columns,
compute the paired (x, y) point set, and render scatter points in the OpenGL canvas.

### 1.1 Data Source Configuration in State

Extend `ScatterPlotStateData` with data source configuration:

```cpp
/// Describes a single axis data source (X or Y)
struct ScatterAxisSource {
    std::string data_key;                            ///< DataManager key (AnalogTimeSeries or TensorData)
    std::optional<std::string> tensor_column_name;   ///< Column name (only for TensorData)
    std::optional<std::size_t> tensor_column_index;  ///< Column index fallback (if no names)
    int time_offset = 0;                             ///< Temporal offset in TimeFrameIndex steps
};

struct ScatterPlotStateData {
    std::string instance_id;
    std::string display_name = "Scatter Plot";
    CorePlotting::ViewStateData view_state;
    HorizontalAxisStateData horizontal_axis;
    VerticalAxisStateData vertical_axis;

    std::optional<ScatterAxisSource> x_source;  ///< X-axis data source
    std::optional<ScatterAxisSource> y_source;  ///< Y-axis data source
};
```

Add signals to `ScatterPlotState`:

| Signal | Trigger |
|--------|---------|
| `xSourceChanged` | X-axis data source key, column, or offset changed |
| `ySourceChanged` | Y-axis data source key, column, or offset changed |

Both source change signals should also emit `stateChanged()` to trigger a scene rebuild.

### 1.2 Data Source UI in Properties Panel

Add to `ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget` in a "Data Sources" section:

**For each axis (X and Y):**

1. **Data type selector** ‚Äî combo box or radio: `AnalogTimeSeries` / `TensorData`
2. **Key combo box** ‚Äî lists all keys of the selected type from DataManager (via `getKeys<AnalogTimeSeries>()` or `getKeys<TensorData>()`)
3. **Column combo box** ‚Äî visible **only** when `TensorData` is selected; populated with column names from `TensorData::getColumnNames()`, falling back to numeric indices (0, 1, 2, ...) if no names are set
4. **Offset spinbox** ‚Äî integer spinbox for temporal offset in `TimeFrameIndex` steps (default: 0)

The combo boxes must be kept in sync with DataManager via the standard observer pattern
(see [Plot Widget Guide](../plot_widget_guide.md)). Register an observer in the constructor
and remove it in the destructor.

### 1.3 Row Type Compatibility Validation

**Status**: Complete.

Implemented as pure-logic validation functions in `Core/SourceCompatibility.hpp/.cpp`
with `ScatterAxisSource` defined in `Core/ScatterAxisSource.hpp`.

**Key types and functions:**

- `ScatterAxisSource` ‚Äî data source descriptor (key, tensor column, temporal offset)
- `ScatterSourceRowType` ‚Äî enum resolving source to one of: `AnalogTimeSeries`,
  `TensorTimeFrameIndex`, `TensorInterval`, `TensorOrdinal`, `Unknown`
- `SourceCompatibilityResult` ‚Äî result struct with `compatible` flag, resolved row types,
  and human-readable `warning_message`
- `resolveSourceRowType(DataManager &, ScatterAxisSource const &)` ‚Äî resolves a single source
- `checkSourceCompatibility(DataManager &, ScatterAxisSource const &, ScatterAxisSource const &)` ‚Äî
  validates the full compatibility matrix

**Compatibility matrix** (unchanged from original design):

| X Source | Y Source | Compatibility |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| `AnalogTimeSeries` | `AnalogTimeSeries` | Always compatible (both `TimeFrameIndex`) |
| `AnalogTimeSeries` | `TensorData` (`TimeFrameIndex` rows) | Compatible ‚Äî 1:1 `TimeFrameIndex` join |
| `TensorData` (`TimeFrameIndex`) | `TensorData` (`TimeFrameIndex`) | Compatible if same `TimeFrame` or joinable |
| `TensorData` (`Interval`) | `TensorData` (`Interval`) | Compatible if same intervals |
| `TensorData` (`Ordinal`) | `TensorData` (`Ordinal`) | Compatible if same row count |

When sources are incompatible, `checkSourceCompatibility` returns a descriptive
`warning_message` suitable for display in the properties panel.

**Tests**: `tests/WhiskerToolbox/Plots/ScatterPlotWidget/SourceCompatibility.test.cpp`
(21 test cases covering all compatible pairings, all incompatible pairings, and missing/unknown sources).

### 1.4 Point Pair Computation

A new data-gathering function builds the (x, y, `TimeFrameIndex`) triples:

```cpp
struct ScatterPointData {
    std::vector<float> x_values;
    std::vector<float> y_values;
    std::vector<TimeFrameIndex> time_indices;  ///< For click-to-navigate and group integration
};

/// Build scatter point data from two axis sources
ScatterPointData buildScatterPoints(
    DataManager const & dm,
    ScatterAxisSource const & x_source,
    ScatterAxisSource const & y_source);
```

**For `AnalogTimeSeries` √ó `AnalogTimeSeries`**: Iterate over the intersection of valid
`TimeFrameIndex` values from both series. Apply temporal offset by shifting the index.

**For `AnalogTimeSeries` √ó `TensorData` (`TimeFrameIndex` rows)**: Join on shared
`TimeFrameIndex` values via the `TensorData`'s `RowDescriptor::timeStorage()`.

### 1.5 Point Rendering

Render the point set via `SceneBuilder::addGlyphs()` ‚Üí `SceneRenderer::render()`, reusing
the glyph rendering infrastructure from [EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/index.qmd).

The `SceneBuilder::addGlyphs()` method collects all `MappedElement` positions into a single
`RenderableGlyphBatch`, which `GlyphRenderer` then uploads as one GPU buffer and renders in
a **single instanced draw call** (`glDrawArraysInstanced` for tick/square/cross, `glDrawArrays`
for circles). This means even large point counts (tens of thousands) result in only one draw
call, identical to how EventPlotWidget handles large spike counts.

There is no need for a dedicated `PointBatchRenderer` ‚Äî the `CorePlotting` abstraction layer
(`MappedElement` ‚Üí `SceneBuilder` ‚Üí `SceneRenderer`) adds negligible overhead (one linear copy
per scene rebuild) while providing spatial index integration for hit testing, entity-to-series
mapping, and glyph customization ‚Äî all of which the scatter plot needs.

### 1.6 Auto-Fit Bounds

After computing point data, scan for min/max X and Y values (with 5% padding) and call
`ScatterPlotState::setXBounds()` and `setYBounds()` to auto-fit the view. This is the same
pattern used by [LinePlotWidget](../LinePlotWidget/index.qmd) for auto-fitting Y bounds
from trial data.

### 1.7 Reference Line Rendering ($y = x$)

Render a $y = x$ diagonal reference line across the visible viewport. This is always available
(not conditional on temporal offset) and useful for comparing two quantities on the same scale.

The line should be rendered as a semi-transparent line in world coordinates using
`SceneBuilder::addPolyLine()` with two vertices at the viewport corners along the diagonal.
Update the line endpoints when the view bounds change (pan/zoom).

Properties panel controls:

- **Show reference line checkbox** ‚Äî toggles the $y = x$ line
- **Reference line style** ‚Äî color and thickness

---

## Phase 2: Double-Click-to-Navigate

**Status**: Complete. Hit testing via `SceneHitTester`/`QuadTree`, navigation signal chain,
and visual highlight are all implemented.

**Goal**: Allow the user to double-click on a scatter point to navigate other time-synced widgets to
that point's `TimeFrameIndex`. This matches the pattern used by [EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/index.qmd)
(`eventDoubleClicked` ‚Üí `setCurrentTime()`).

### 2.1 Point Hit Testing

Hit testing is already provided by the shared `CorePlotting::SceneHitTester` class
(`CorePlotting/Interaction/SceneHitTester.hpp`), which queries the `QuadTree<EntityId>`
spatial index built automatically by `SceneBuilder::addGlyphs()`. This is the same system
used by [EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/index.qmd) for double-click-to-navigate and
click-to-select.

Implement hit testing in `ScatterPlotOpenGLWidget`:

1. Store a `CorePlotting::SceneHitTester _hit_tester` member
2. On `mouseDoubleClickEvent`: convert screen position to world coordinates via `screenToWorld()`
3. Convert the pixel tolerance to world units (matching the EventPlotWidget pattern):

```cpp
float world_per_pixel_x = (view_state.x_max - view_state.x_min) /
                          (widget_width * view_state.x_zoom);
float world_per_pixel_y = (view_state.y_max - view_state.y_min) /
                          (widget_height * view_state.y_zoom);
float world_tolerance = 8.0f * std::max(world_per_pixel_x, world_per_pixel_y);
```

4. Call `_hit_tester.queryQuadTree(world_x, world_y, _scene)` ‚Äî no `LayoutResponse` needed
   since the scatter plot has no series regions
5. Check `result.hasHit()` and extract `result.entity_id` to look up the
   associated `TimeFrameIndex` from the cached `ScatterPointData`

The `QuadTree` is built as part of the normal scene construction: `SceneBuilder::addGlyphs()`
inserts every `MappedElement` into the spatial index during the same traversal that populates
the GPU buffer. No additional data structures or rebuild logic are required.

This approach scales efficiently to large point counts via the QuadTree's `O(log n)` nearest-
neighbor lookup (with `MAX_DEPTH = 8` and `MAX_POINTS_PER_NODE = 16`).

### 2.2 Navigation Signal

On double-click hit, use the `EntityId` from the `HitTestResult` to look up the
corresponding `TimeFrameIndex`:

```cpp
auto result = _hit_tester.queryQuadTree(world_x, world_y, _scene);
if (result.hasHit() && result.entity_id.has_value()) {
    // Map EntityId back to TimeFrameIndex via cached ScatterPointData
    auto it = _entity_to_time_index.find(*result.entity_id);
    if (it != _entity_to_time_index.end()) {
        TimeFrameIndex tfi = it->second;
        TimePosition position = time_frame->getTimeAtIndex(tfi);
        emit pointDoubleClicked(position);
    }
}
```

The `_entity_to_time_index` map is built alongside the `MappedElement` list during
scene construction, mapping each point's `EntityId` to its `TimeFrameIndex`.

The parent `ScatterPlotWidget` forwards this as `timePositionSelected(TimePosition)`, which
the registration already wires to `EditorRegistry::setCurrentTime()`.

This is identical to the [EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/index.qmd) pattern
(`eventDoubleClicked` ‚Üí `setCurrentTime()`).

### 2.3 Visual Feedback on Navigation

Highlight the most recently navigated-to point with a larger size and different color. This
is implemented as a **second `addGlyphs()` call** after the main point batch, using a
different `GlyphStyle`. Because `GlyphRenderer` iterates all batches in order and each batch
has its own `u_point_size` / `u_glyph_size` uniform, the highlight batch renders on top of
the normal batch at the correct size ‚Äî no shader changes required.

```cpp
// Main point batch (all points)
GlyphStyle normal_style;
normal_style.size = 5.0f;
normal_style.color = glm::vec4(0.2f, 0.53f, 1.0f, 0.8f);  // blue
builder.addGlyphs("scatter_points", all_elements, normal_style);

// Highlight batch (single navigated-to point)
if (_navigated_index.has_value()) {
    GlyphStyle highlight_style;
    highlight_style.size = 9.0f;  // larger
    highlight_style.color = glm::vec4(1.0f, 0.8f, 0.0f, 1.0f);  // yellow
    builder.addGlyphs("scatter_highlight", {all_elements[*_navigated_index]}, highlight_style);
}
```

Store `_navigated_index` (a `std::optional<std::size_t>`) in `ScatterPlotOpenGLWidget`.
Clear it when the data source changes or a new source is selected.

No tooltip is included here ‚Äî see Phase 8 (Hover Tooltip) for deferred tooltip functionality.

---

## Phase 3: Polygon Selection and Group Integration

**Status**: Not started. Depends on Phase 1 (point data) and the shared polygon selection infrastructure.

**Goal**: Allow the user to select multiple points via polygon (lasso) selection and add their
associated `TimeFrameIndex` entities to an entity group.

### 3.1 Polygon Interaction (Existing Infrastructure)

The polygon drawing interaction is already implemented as a shared component:
`CorePlotting::Interaction::PolygonInteractionController` (in
`CorePlotting/Interaction/PolygonInteractionController.hpp/.cpp`). This controller provides:

- Click-based polygon creation: `start()` ‚Üí repeated `addVertex()` ‚Üí `complete()`
- Cursor preview via `updateCursorPosition()` (shows edge from last vertex to cursor)
- Auto-close by clicking near the first vertex (configurable threshold)
- Vertex drag mode for modifying existing polygons
- Undo via `removeLastVertex()`
- `GlyphPreview` output for rendering the polygon outline during construction
- Configurable colors, stroke width, vertex size

The ScatterPlotWidget needs to:

1. **Instantiate** a `PolygonInteractionController` in `ScatterPlotOpenGLWidget`
2. **Wire mouse events**: Ctrl+Click ‚Üí `addVertex()`, mouse move ‚Üí `updateCursorPosition()`,
   Escape ‚Üí `cancel()`, Ctrl+Double-click ‚Üí `complete()`
3. **Render the preview** via `getPreview()` using the existing `PreviewRenderer`
4. **On completion**, extract `getVertices()` and run point-in-polygon selection

### 3.1.1 Point-in-Polygon Selection

A selection utility performs the actual point containment test after the polygon is closed:

```cpp
namespace CorePlotting::Selection {

struct PolygonSelectionResult {
    std::vector<std::size_t> selected_indices;  ///< Indices into the point array
};

/// Find all points inside the polygon (ray-casting algorithm)
PolygonSelectionResult selectPointsInPolygon(
    std::span<glm::vec2 const> polygon_vertices,
    std::span<float const> x_values,
    std::span<float const> y_values);

}  // namespace CorePlotting::Selection
```

This is a pure geometry function with no Qt or OpenGL dependency, placed in
`CorePlotting/Selection/` for sharing with
[TemporalProjectionViewWidget](../TemporalProjectionViewWidget/index.qmd).

### 3.2 Polygon Drawing Interaction

In `ScatterPlotOpenGLWidget`, wire the existing `PolygonInteractionController`:

| Input | Action | Controller Method |
|-------|--------|-------------------|
| Ctrl + Click | Begin polygon / add vertex | `start()` or `addVertex()` |
| Mouse move (while active) | Update preview line | `updateCursorPosition()` |
| Ctrl + Double-click | Close polygon and execute selection | `complete()` |
| Escape | Cancel polygon drawing | `cancel()` |
| Backspace (while drawing) | Undo last vertex | `removeLastVertex()` |
| Click near first vertex | Auto-close polygon | `addVertex()` returns `ClosedPolygon` |
| Shift + Ctrl + Click | Remove mode (deselect points in polygon) | New polygon in subtract mode |

The `PolygonInteractionController::getPreview()` returns a `GlyphPreview` with `Type::Polygon`
that the existing `PreviewRenderer` can render. The preview includes polygon edges, vertex
markers, and a closure line from the last vertex to the cursor.

### 3.3 Selection State

Track the current selection in state:

```cpp
struct ScatterPlotStateData {
    // ... existing fields ...
    std::vector<std::size_t> selected_indices;  ///< Indices into the point array
};
```

Selected points should be rendered with a distinct visual style (e.g., highlighted color,
larger glyph, or a ring) to distinguish them from unselected points.

### 3.4 Selection ‚Üí Group Integration

When points are selected, the properties panel should show group management controls:

1. **"Add to Group" button** ‚Äî enabled when a non-empty selection exists
2. **Group combo box** ‚Äî lists existing entity groups from `EntityRegistry`
3. **"New Group" button** ‚Äî creates a new group and adds the selected entities

The workflow:

```
User draws polygon over scatter points
        ‚Üì
selectPointsInPolygon() ‚Üí selected_indices
        ‚Üì
Map selected_indices ‚Üí TimeFrameIndex values via ScatterPointData::time_indices
        ‚Üì
Look up EntityIDs for those TimeFrameIndex values
        ‚Üì
User selects/creates a group ‚Üí EntityRegistry::addToGroup(entity_ids)
```

### 3.5 Shared Group Management Panel

This group management pattern is shared across multiple widgets. As proposed in the
[LinePlotWidget Roadmap](../LinePlotWidget/roadmap.qmd) Phase 1.3, a shared
`GroupManagementPanel` widget should be factored into `Plots/Common/` (or a higher-level
shared widget location):

| Widget | What Gets Added to Group | Source of Selection |
|--------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| **ScatterPlotWidget** | `TimeFrameIndex` EntityIDs | Polygon selection |
| **[LinePlotWidget](../LinePlotWidget/index.qmd)** | Alignment event EntityIDs (trials) | Line selection stroke |
| **[TemporalProjectionViewWidget](../TemporalProjectionViewWidget/index.qmd)** | Line/Point EntityIDs | Line selection stroke / polygon selection |
| **DataViewerWidget** | Data entity EntityIDs | (future) row/range selection |

Initial implementation can be done inline in `ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget` and then
refactored when the second widget needs the same functionality.

---

## Phase 4: Glyph Customization

**Status**: Not started. Depends on Phase 1 (point rendering).

**Goal**: Allow the user to configure the visual appearance of scatter points (glyph shape,
size, color), using the shared `CorePlotting::GlyphStyleData` infrastructure from
`CorePlotting/DataTypes/GlyphStyleData.hpp` and `GlyphStyleControls` from
`Plots/Common/GlyphStyleWidget/`.

### 4.1 Glyph Options in State

Use the shared `CorePlotting::GlyphStyleData` struct instead of defining a widget-specific
glyph type enum:

```cpp
#include "CorePlotting/DataTypes/GlyphStyleData.hpp"

struct ScatterPlotStateData {
    // ... existing fields ...
    CorePlotting::GlyphStyleData glyph_style; // Uses shared GlyphType enum
};
```

The `CorePlotting::GlyphType` enum already covers Circle, Square, Tick, Cross, Diamond, and
Triangle. Additional per-widget options like border settings can be added as separate fields.

### 4.2 Shared Glyph Rendering Infrastructure

The shared glyph infrastructure is now implemented:

| Component | Location | Status |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| `CorePlotting::GlyphType` enum | `CorePlotting/DataTypes/GlyphStyleData.hpp` | **Complete** |
| `CorePlotting::GlyphStyleData` struct | `CorePlotting/DataTypes/GlyphStyleData.hpp` | **Complete** |
| `CorePlotting::toRenderableGlyphType()` | `CorePlotting/DataTypes/GlyphStyleConversion.hpp` | **Complete** |
| `CorePlotting::hexColorToVec4()` | `CorePlotting/DataTypes/GlyphStyleConversion.hpp` | **Complete** |
| `GlyphStyleState` | `Plots/Common/GlyphStyleWidget/Core/GlyphStyleState.hpp` | **Complete** |
| `GlyphStyleControls` | `Plots/Common/GlyphStyleWidget/GlyphStyleControls.hpp` | **Complete** |

Already integrated into: TemporalProjectionViewWidget, OnionSkinViewWidget, EventPlotWidget.

### 4.3 Properties Panel Controls

Embed `GlyphStyleControls` from `Plots/Common/GlyphStyleWidget/` in a "Glyph Options"
collapsible section of `ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget`, following the pattern used by
TemporalProjectionViewWidget and OnionSkinViewWidget:

```cpp
#include "Plots/Common/GlyphStyleWidget/GlyphStyleControls.hpp"

auto * controls = new GlyphStyleControls(state->glyphStyleState(), parent);
```

Additional scatter-specific controls (border, selection style) can be added alongside.

### 4.4 Selected Point Style

Selected points (from Phase 3) use the same **two-batch `addGlyphs()` pattern** introduced
in Phase 2.3, generalized to an arbitrary number of selected indices.

Because `RenderableGlyphBatch::size` is a per-batch uniform (not per-instance), the only
way to have different sizes for selected vs. unselected points is to issue two separate
batches. Per-instance **color** is already supported (each point's `glm::vec4` is uploaded
as an instanced VBO attribute), but since we need a size difference too, two batches is
the correct approach regardless.

```cpp
// Batch 1: unselected points (exclusion pass)
std::vector<MappedElement> unselected, selected;
for (std::size_t i = 0; i < _scatter_data.size(); ++i) {
    if (_selected_indices.contains(i))
        selected.push_back(all_elements[i]);
    else
        unselected.push_back(all_elements[i]);
}

GlyphStyle base_style{.size = glyph_style.size, .color = base_color};
builder.addGlyphs("scatter_points", unselected, base_style);

// Batch 2: selected points rendered on top, larger and highlighted
if (!selected.empty()) {
    GlyphStyle sel_style{.size = base_style.size * 1.6f,
                         .color = glm::vec4(1.0f, 0.2f, 0.2f, 1.0f)};  // red
    builder.addGlyphs("scatter_selected", selected, sel_style);
}
```

This is efficient: only two GPU draw calls regardless of how many points are selected.
The selected point style (color, size multiplier) can be exposed in the properties panel
as part of glyph customization (Phase 4.3).

---

## Phase 5: Temporal Offset for Outlier Detection

**Status**: Not started. Depends on Phase 1 (data source configuration).

**Goal**: Allow plotting a signal against its own time-shifted version to identify points
where values change too rapidly.

### 5.1 Use Case

Plotting `MaskArea(t)` vs `MaskArea(t-1)` produces a scatter where:

- Points near the diagonal ($y = x$) represent slow changes
- Points far from the diagonal represent rapid transitions (potential outliers)

This is a common quality-control visualization for time series data.

### 5.2 Implementation

The `ScatterAxisSource::time_offset` field (defined in Phase 1.1) handles this directly.
When the user sets `time_offset = -1` for the Y-axis source:

1. For each `TimeFrameIndex` $t$, the X value is $x(t)$ and the Y value is $x(t + \text{offset}) = x(t-1)$
2. Points where the offset pushes $t$ outside valid range are excluded
3. The `TimeFrameIndex` stored per point is the "primary" index (X-axis, no offset)

### 5.3 Reference Line

The $y = x$ reference line from Phase 1.7 is already available for this use case. When both
axes use the same source key and one has a temporal offset, the reference line immediately
shows which points represent rapid changes (far from the diagonal) vs. slow changes (near it).

### 5.4 Properties Panel Controls

- **Offset spinbox** ‚Äî per-axis integer spinbox (already in Phase 1.2)

---

## Phase 6: DataManager Observer and Stale Key Handling

**Status**: Partially complete. Observer registration (6.1) and combo box population (6.2) are implemented as part of Phase 1.2. Stale key handling (6.3) and dedicated tests (6.4) remain.

**Goal**: Register a DataManager observer to keep combo boxes current and handle deleted data keys.

### 6.1 Observer Registration

Following the pattern from [Plot Widget Guide](../plot_widget_guide.md):

```cpp
ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget::ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget(
    std::shared_ptr<ScatterPlotState> state,
    std::shared_ptr<DataManager> data_manager,
    QWidget * parent)
    : QWidget(parent), _data_manager(std::move(data_manager))
{
    if (_data_manager) {
        _dm_observer_id = _data_manager->addObserver([this]() {
            _populateComboBoxes();
        });
    }
}

ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget::~ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget()
{
    if (_data_manager && _dm_observer_id != -1) {
        _data_manager->removeObserver(_dm_observer_id);
    }
}
```

### 6.2 Combo Box Population

When the observer fires:

1. Re-query `DataManager::getKeys<AnalogTimeSeries>()` and `DataManager::getKeys<TensorData>()`
2. Rebuild X and Y key combo boxes, preserving current selection if still valid
3. If a `TensorData` key is selected, re-query its column names and rebuild the column combo box

### 6.3 Stale Key Handling

If the currently configured X or Y source key is deleted from the DataManager:

- Clear the axis source in state (`x_source = std::nullopt`)
- Clear the rendered scatter points
- Update the properties panel to indicate no source selected

### 6.4 Tests

Add `ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget.test.cpp` following the common test pattern from
`tests/WhiskerToolbox/Plots/Common/PlotWidgetCommonTests.hpp`:

- Combo box population from DataManager
- Observer refresh on data addition/removal
- Destruction cleanup (no dangling observer)
- Stale key handling

---

## Phase 7: Feature Coloring via TensorData

**Status**: Design phase. Depends on Phase 1 (rendering) and the shared colormap infrastructure.

**Goal**: Color scatter points by an external feature value from a `TensorData` column, enabling
visual grouping by continuous or categorical features.

### 7.1 Color Configuration

```cpp
struct ScatterColorConfig {
    std::string color_mode = "fixed";    ///< "fixed" or "by_feature"

    // For feature-based coloring
    std::string tensor_key;              ///< DataManager key for the color TensorData
    std::string column_name;             ///< Column within the TensorData
    std::string colormap = "viridis";    ///< Colormap name
    double color_min = 0.0;
    double color_max = 1.0;
    bool use_auto_range = true;
};
```

### 7.2 Shared Colormap Infrastructure

This depends on the colormap infrastructure proposed in the [HeatmapWidget Roadmap](../HeatmapWidget/roadmap.qmd)
and referenced across multiple widget roadmaps:

```cpp
namespace CorePlotting::Colormaps {
    using ColormapFunction = std::function<glm::vec4(float t)>;
    ColormapFunction getColormap(ColormapPreset preset);
    glm::vec4 mapValue(ColormapFunction const & cmap, float value, float vmin, float vmax);
}
```

The colormap system in `CorePlotting/Colormaps/` is shared across:

| Widget | Usage |
|--------|-------|
| **ScatterPlotWidget** | Per-point color from feature value |
| **[LinePlotWidget](../LinePlotWidget/roadmap.qmd)** | Per-trial line color from feature value |
| **[EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/roadmap.qmd)** | Per-trial event color |
| **[HeatmapWidget](../HeatmapWidget/roadmap.qmd)** | Heatmap cell color |

### 7.3 Properties Panel

Add to `ScatterPlotPropertiesWidget` in a "Point Coloring" collapsible section:

1. **Color mode combo** ‚Äî Fixed / By Feature
2. **TensorData combo box** ‚Äî lists `TensorData` keys from DataManager
3. **Column combo box** ‚Äî shows columns of the selected `TensorData`
4. **Colormap combo** ‚Äî colormap preset selection
5. **Range controls** ‚Äî auto/manual min/max spinboxes

---

## Dependency Graph

```
Phase 1: Data Source Selection + Rendering + Reference Line (COMPLETE)
    ‚Üì
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Phase 2: Double-Click-to-Navigate
    ‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Phase 8: Hover Tooltip (deferred; depends on Phase 2 hit-testing)
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Phase 3: Polygon Selection + Groups
    ‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ uses: PolygonInteractionController (already implemented)
    ‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ needs: CorePlotting::Selection::selectPointsInPolygon (new)
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Phase 4: Glyph Customization
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Phase 5: Temporal Offset (offset mechanism implemented in Phase 1; UI/UX refinement needed)
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Phase 6: DataManager Observer (observer + combo population done; stale key handling remains)

Phase 7: Feature Coloring
    ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ depends on: Phase 1 (rendering) ‚Äî COMPLETE
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ depends on: CorePlotting::Colormaps (shared with HeatmapWidget, EventPlotWidget, LinePlotWidget)
```

Phase 1 is the foundation ‚Äî all other phases depend on it. Phases 2‚Äì6 are largely independent
of each other and can be implemented in any order after Phase 1. Phase 6 (DataManager observer)
can also be started in parallel with Phase 1 since it only involves the properties panel.

## Shared Components Summary

Components that should be factored into shared locations rather than implemented inline:

| Component | Proposed Location | Used By |
|-----------|-------------------|---------|
| `selectPointsInPolygon` | `CorePlotting/Selection/` | ScatterPlotWidget, [TemporalProjectionViewWidget](../TemporalProjectionViewWidget/index.qmd) |
| `GroupManagementPanel` | `Plots/Common/` | ScatterPlotWidget, [LinePlotWidget](../LinePlotWidget/roadmap.qmd), [TemporalProjectionViewWidget](../TemporalProjectionViewWidget/index.qmd) |
| `Colormaps` | `CorePlotting/Colormaps/` | ScatterPlotWidget, [HeatmapWidget](../HeatmapWidget/roadmap.qmd), [EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/roadmap.qmd), [LinePlotWidget](../LinePlotWidget/roadmap.qmd) |


---

## Phase 8: Hover Tooltip (Deferred)

**Status**: Deferred. Depends on Phase 2 (hit testing infrastructure).

**Goal**: Show a tooltip with point coordinates and `TimeFrameIndex` when the user hovers
over a scatter point for a brief dwell time.

**Complexity note**: Hover tooltips require a debounce/dwell timer to avoid flickering
on fast mouse movements, careful coordinate conversion, and Qt tooltip lifecycle management.
These concerns are separated from the simpler double-click navigation (Phase 2) to keep
that phase focused.

### 8.1 Dwell Timer

Use a `QTimer` (single-shot, ~400 ms) that resets on every `mouseMoveEvent`. When the timer
fires without a reset, perform a hit test at the last known cursor position using the same
`SceneHitTester` infrastructure from Phase 2.1:

```cpp
// In ScatterPlotOpenGLWidget
QTimer * _hover_timer = nullptr;   // single-shot, ~400 ms
QPoint _last_cursor_pos;

void mouseMoveEvent(QMouseEvent * event) override {
    _last_cursor_pos = event->pos();
    _hover_timer->start(400);       // restart on every move
    // ... existing pan logic ...
}

void _onHoverTimerFired() {
    QPointF world = screenToWorld(_last_cursor_pos);
    auto result = _hit_tester.queryQuadTree(
        static_cast<float>(world.x()),
        static_cast<float>(world.y()),
        _scene);
    if (result.hasHit() && result.entity_id.has_value()) {
        // build tooltip string from result.world_x, result.world_y, entity_id
        // and show via QToolTip::showText()
    } else {
        QToolTip::hideText();
    }
}
```

### 8.2 Tooltip Content

The tooltip should display:

- X and Y values (formatted to a reasonable precision)
- The `TimeFrameIndex` value
- Optionally: the absolute time value (`time_frame->getTimeAtIndex(tfi)`)

### 8.3 Interaction with Pan/Zoom

Hide the tooltip immediately when a pan or zoom gesture begins, and cancel the pending
timer to avoid a tooltip appearing mid-pan.

---

## Design Decisions

1. **Point count scalability**: The `SceneBuilder::addGlyphs()` ‚Üí `GlyphRenderer` pipeline uses
   instanced rendering (single GPU draw call for all points in a batch). This is the same path
   EventPlotWidget uses for large spike counts and scales well to tens of thousands of points.
   No dedicated `PointBatchRenderer` is needed.

2. **Polygon interaction**: Uses the existing `PolygonInteractionController` from
   `CorePlotting/Interaction/`. This shared controller handles click-based polygon creation,
   vertex drag modification, cursor preview, and auto-close detection.

3. **Reference line**: Only $y = x$ reference line for now (Phase 1.7). Can be generalized to
   arbitrary linear reference lines ($y = ax + b$) in a future phase if needed.

4. **Hit testing via shared QuadTree**: Point hit testing uses the existing `SceneHitTester`
   (`CorePlotting/Interaction/SceneHitTester.hpp`) which queries the `QuadTree<EntityId>`
   spatial index built automatically by `SceneBuilder::addGlyphs()`. This is the same
   infrastructure used by [EventPlotWidget](../EventPlotWidget/index.qmd) for double-click
   navigation and click-to-select. The QuadTree provides `O(log n)` nearest-neighbor lookup,
   scaling well to large point counts without widget-specific spatial indexing code. Navigation
   uses `mouseDoubleClickEvent`, matching EventPlotWidget. Hover tooltips are deferred to
   Phase 8 because they require a debounce timer and additional Qt tooltip lifecycle management
   that would complicate the simpler Phase 2 implementation.

5. **Two-batch rendering for selection/highlight**: `GlyphRenderer` renders each `RenderableGlyphBatch` in order, with its own `u_point_size` / `u_glyph_size` uniform. Per-instance color is already supported by the shader (`a_instance_color` / `a_color` VBO attribute), but per-instance size is not ‚Äî size is a per-batch uniform. Therefore, rendering selected/highlighted points at a different size requires a second `addGlyphs()` call. This results in exactly two GPU draw calls (one for unselected, one for selected) regardless of the selection count, with no shader changes needed.

6. **Single dataset per plot**: Each ScatterPlotWidget displays a single X/Y data source pair.
   Users can open multiple scatter plot instances via the Analysis Dashboard for side-by-side
   comparison of different variable pairs.

# See Also

- [ScatterPlotWidget Documentation](index.qmd) ‚Äî Full architecture and feature documentation
- [Plot Widget Guide](../plot_widget_guide.md) ‚Äî Shared architecture, axis types, DataManager integration
- [LinePlotWidget Roadmap](../LinePlotWidget/roadmap.qmd) ‚Äî Group integration pattern (Phase 1), feature coloring (Phase 3)
- [EventPlotWidget Roadmap](../EventPlotWidget/roadmap.qmd) ‚Äî TensorData feature coloring/sorting (Phase 5)
- [HeatmapWidget Roadmap](../HeatmapWidget/roadmap.qmd) ‚Äî Colormap infrastructure (Phase 2)
- [TemporalProjectionViewWidget](../TemporalProjectionViewWidget/index.qmd) ‚Äî Polygon selection with PointData, shared rendering
